paragraphs.text: Hello, a big player in video games. I often wonder what would happen to my video games if Ubisoft (or other) went bankrupt? Currently, it is impossible to play a Ubisoft, Steam, Epic Games or other game without having to connect to his account. We didn’t have a problem when you had to return a product key, making the game play for as long as we had it. Today, I think it is imperative to change this model and return to a product key system that made it possible both to avoid theft but also to leave the player free to use it and did not make it dependent on the existence of the company. Moreover, what is arduous is the obligation to update the game to play. Sometimes you do not play it for 1 week and you have to re-load a rather heavy update with a connection of 80 mbps, which can take 1 to 2. (I am thinking in particular of GTA V). So pay you a game to spend your time updating it. We should be able not to update the game and even to be able to go back to a previous version (which might not present bugs for example). I was playing a game called Anno 2070, today I can’t play it anymore, because Ubisoft actually had an update that had everything planed and they also brought the servers off and so today, I can no longer play it, or if I arrive there I have to play solo. However, it would have been more cumbersome on the part of those large platforms to add a ‘LAN’ option which allows the computer itself to host the game server in order to play several of them without being dependent on Ubisoft’s servers. This would also ensure an indefinite longevity for the game. The Lethal Company game is a good example of this system. With regard to the installations, I would dream of being able to purchase again installation CD-ROMs. Today, on PCs, there are no longer CD-ROMs of PC video games. The disadvantage is that today we have become dependent on the platform and we need an internet connection to install (or even play) the game. Physical facilities did not depend on this and drastically liberalised the internet bandwidth at home. Today, I am thinking of no longer buying video games because the fear that they will disappear overnight is great (too great). With regard to Operating Systems (especially mobile), it would be true that the user would be able to choose which one he wants. Since the start of the mobile, we have been either with iOS or Android (Googleised version), it is becoming increasingly difficult to change system (I think Samsung is revoking the guarantee if you change operating systems or blocking system apps for life). The more years pass and the more we become dependent on these big companies and imprisoned in their systems. I see it in particular with Google and its Play Store, today, I no longer have a Google account (by choice), I can no longer install my CBC bank’s app (this is serious) because it is available only on the Play Store and because it verifies that it has been installed from that store and I must have a gode account to install it. As regards sanitised (and other) applications, I strongly criticise the fact that it is not possible to export and import these data in a standard format to change applications. It is time for the internet and electronic devices to serve citizens again and not the economic interests of a few big companies. As an IT developer and student, these are questions that I very much ask myself naturally, many people do not really realise what all this means. I feel that I have lost a lot of freedom on the internet and with my devices in recent years.
